explanation.rule: The prefix over- is added to verbs and adjectives to mean 'too much', 'above', or 'beyond a normal level'. It often suggests excess or an action carried further than appropriate.

groups.note: Means 'too much', 'excessively', or 'above'; often implies excess or going beyond a reasonable limit

examples.definition: containing too many people or things
to think something is larger, better, or more important than it really is
to respond more strongly or emotionally than the situation requires
to affect someone so strongly that they cannot cope
to fail to notice or consider something; to have a view over an area

wordForms.definition: containing too many people or things
wordForms.example: The stadium was overcrowded and thousands of fans were turned away.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a large number of people gathered together

wordForms.definition: to do something to an excessive degree
wordForms.example: Don't overdo the exercise if your back is still sore.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to carry out an action or task

wordForms.definition: to think something is larger, better, or more significant than it really is
wordForms.example: We overestimated the number of people who would attend the event.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to form an approximate judgement about size, value, or amount

wordForms.definition: to respond more strongly or emotionally than the situation requires
wordForms.example: Try not to overreact to every small setback at work.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to respond to something that has happened

wordForms.definition: to describe something as greater or more important than it really is
wordForms.example: It would be hard to overstate the importance of this decision.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to express or declare in words

wordForms.definition: to affect someone so powerfully that they cannot cope
wordForms.example: She felt overwhelmed by the amount of work she had to do.

wordForms.definition: to succeed in dealing with or controlling a problem or difficulty
wordForms.example: With practice, she managed to overcome her fear of public speaking.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to arrive at or reach a place or state

wordForms.definition: to put too great a load or amount of work on someone or something
wordForms.example: The manager tends to overload his staff with too many tasks at once.
wordForms.rootDefinition: a quantity that is carried or transported

wordForms.definition: to fail to notice or deal with something; to have a view over an area
wordForms.example: The inspector overlooked several important details in the report.
wordForms.rootDefinition: to direct one's gaze; to watch over

wordForms.definition: to place too high a value or importance on something
wordForms.example: Analysts warned that investors were overvaluing the company's shares.
wordForms.rootDefinition: the importance or worth assigned to something

multipleChoiceExercises.sentence: The city centre was dangerously ___ during the summer festival.
Don't ___ the salt in the sauce – a little goes a long way.
She tends to ___ when things go wrong, even slightly.
It would be impossible to ___ the damage caused by the storm.
The new employee felt ___ by all the responsibilities given on her first day.
The inspector ___ several key safety issues during his visit.

transformExercises.sentence: Many people ___ how difficult it is to learn a second language. (ESTIMATE)
She finally ___ her anxiety about speaking in front of large groups. (COME)
The server was ___ with requests and crashed during the peak hour. (LOAD)
Property in the capital is widely considered to be ___ at the moment. (VALUE)
The beaches were ___ during the heatwave and had to be closed. (CROWD)
